description.en: Return the value (in fractional seconds) of a performance counter, i.e. a clock with the highest available resolution to measure a short duration. It does include time elapsed during sleep. The clock is the same for all processes. The reference point of the returned value is undefined, so that only the difference between the results of two calls is valid. CPython implementation detail: On CPython, use the same clock as time.monotonic() and is a monotonic clock, i.e. a clock that cannot go bac...

description.en: Similar to perf_counter(), but return time as nanoseconds. Added in version 3.7.

info.excerpt: Similar to perf_counter(), but return time as nanoseconds. Added in version 3.7.

description.en: Return the value (in fractional seconds) of the sum of the system and user CPU time of the current process. It does not include time elapsed during sleep. It is process-wide by definition. The reference point of the returned value is undefined, so that only the difference between the results of two calls is valid. Use process_time_ns() to avoid the precision loss caused by the float type. Added in version 3.3.

description.en: Similar to process_time() but return time as nanoseconds. Added in version 3.7.

info.excerpt: Similar to process_time() but return time as nanoseconds. Added in version 3.7.

description.en: Return the clk_id of the thread-specific CPU-time clock for the specified thread_id. Use threading.get_ident() or the ident attribute of threading.Thread objects to get a suitable value for thread_id. Warning Passing an invalid or expired thread_id may result in undefined behavior, such as segmentation fault. Availability: Unix See the man page for pthread_getcpuclockid(3) for further information. Added in version 3.7.
